You are a DIRTY-ROOM integrator for the tamarin-rs relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. Read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md and the unit-D sections of INTEGRATION_REPORT.md (waves 2-4 scoped this swap; the wave-4 D integrator's section explains why it stopped: the verdict taxonomy was incomplete).
RULES: adapters and extraction only — NEVER transplant ported logic into clean (headerless) files; clean vendored sources get mechanical fixes only. A clean file acquiring a GPL header from scripts/gen_license_headers.py is a provenance violation: stop and report. If a gap still blocks, KEEP the ported path + report precisely. Comments describe current code only. No git commits. Append a dated section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md; return a plain-text summary. If you must stop partway, leave the tree BUILDING AND GREEN.

THE TAXONOMY IS NOW COMPLETE: round 6-cont closed the --diff family (LemmaSide, projected falsified/incomplete, side independence, no-skip listing) and round 7 (console cluster workspace, audit passed) added the last non-diff phrase — LemmaResult::AnalysisCannotBeFinished ("analysis cannot be finished (reducible operators in subterms)"), proven UNIFORM across trace kinds, no advisory line, composing with --diff prefixes. Round 7 also established by systematic probing that bound exhaustion renders "analysis incomplete", and that no further phrase is batch-reachable — in particular the ported run.rs's "analysis undetermined"/"proof has been invalidated" render arms are NOT reachable from any batch run of the reference. A ported unit test pinning RS-only render strings for HS-batch-unreachable states pins invented behavior, not reference parity — you may retire those pins WITH a written justification in the report (this is not weakening a byte assertion against the reference; cite the round-7 negative-taxonomy evidence in console/workspace/BEHAVIOR.md).

STEPS:
1. Re-sync ALL round-7 clean cli modules from /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/console/workspace/cli-clean/src/ into crates/tamarin-prover/src/cli/ (mechanical fixes only, headerless) + new fixtures; regenerate tests/console_split_parity.rs from the clean test-suite delta (55 -> more).
2. Make the run-driver contract changes in the PORTED files (allowed, they stay headered): defer value-validation forcing until after the maude preamble exactly as the reference does (wave-2 report has the live evidence: --bound=x emits preamble THEN error); route structural-error one-liners bare to stderr and validation envelopes (error + full help) to stdout via the clean errors module; recover the stop-on-trace absent/present distinction via an options re-parse; render batch summaries through the clean framing (full taxonomy incl. AnalysisCannotBeFinished + diff projections) and stream via the clean incremental emitter (progress as produced — the consumer controls flush timing).
3. Route argument tokenization/typing through the clean typed args with a thin mapping into the existing Args consumer struct (the struct definition may stay ported/headered; the goal is deleting the ported TOKENIZER/VALIDATOR bodies).
4. CORPUS GATE before deleting: curated theory set exercising every verdict class (use the console cluster's round-6/7 probe inputs: YellowTest, YellowDiffTest, diff_false/diff_asym/two_lemma probes, wf-warning + lemma theories, bounded --prove, multi-theory) — run reference vs RS with SPLIT streams (the cluster's split harness pattern); require byte-equality modulo build metadata. Plus cli_e2e + console_split_parity + a live --bound=x / unknown-flag / no-input spot-check triplet.
5. When green, DELETE the ported parse_args/validation bodies and the ported summary/framing assembly in run.rs. The three Rust-only flags (--processors/--maude-processes/--data-dir) keep working through clean typed args.
FINAL VALIDATION: cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-parser -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-prover -p tamarin-server; wellformedness fixture suite; scripts/gen_license_headers.py then --check (0 stale). Report the header-count delta and any author citations that disappeared.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

1	//! Typed argument parsing with value validation.
2	//!
3	//! [`parse_args`] layers a typed surface over the structural [`crate::parse`]:
4	//! `parse_args(argv) -> Result<Parsed, ParseError>`. On success it yields either a
5	//! help/version request or a fully typed [`Args`] with integers parsed, enums
6	//! resolved, and documented defaults applied. On a value-validation failure it
7	//! yields a [`ParseError`] whose text and stream reproduce the reference binary's
8	//! output byte-for-byte (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §11).
9	//!
10	//! Only eight flags are value-validated (the rest accept any value). Validation
11	//! fires only for a *present* value (`=value`, `--oj=…`, or short-attached `-b5`);
12	//! a bare flag never fails. Repeated flags follow last-wins. When several flags
13	//! carry an invalid value, a fixed order — independent of command-line order —
14	//! decides which error surfaces:
15	//!
16	//! `stop-on-trace > bound > partial-evaluation > open-chains > saturation >
17	//! output-module > derivcheck-timeout > replication-bound`.
18	
19	use crate::errors::{self, ParseError};
20	use crate::modes::Mode;
21	use crate::parse::{parse, Command, Options};
22	
23	/// The `--stop-on-trace` method (matched case-insensitively).
24	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
25	pub enum StopOnTrace {
26	    Dfs,
27	    Bfs,
28	    SeqDfs,
29	    Sorry,
30	    None,
31	}
32	
33	/// The `--partial-evaluation` mode (matched case-insensitively).
34	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
35	pub enum PartialEval {
36	    Summary,
37	    Verbose,
38	}
39	
40	/// The `-m --output-module` target (matched case-sensitively).
41	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
42	pub enum OutputModule {
43	    SpthyTyped,
44	    Spthy,
45	    Msr,
46	    ProverifEquiv,
47	    Proverif,
48	    Deepsec,
49	}
50	
51	/// Documented default for `--open-chains`.
52	pub const OPEN_CHAINS_DEFAULT: i64 = 10;
53	/// Documented default for `--saturation`.
54	pub const SATURATION_DEFAULT: i64 = 5;
55	/// Documented default for `--derivcheck-timeout`.
56	pub const DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT: i64 = 5;
57	
58	/// A fully typed, validated command-line, with defaults applied.
59	///
60	/// Fields cover every flag the mode tables model. Flags irrelevant to the active
61	/// [`Mode`] simply hold their defaults. Bare-vs-valued is preserved only where it
62	/// is semantically load-bearing (the repeatable selectors); other single-valued
63	/// string flags collapse an absent or bare occurrence to `None`.
64	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
65	pub struct Args {
66	    pub mode: Mode,
67	    /// FILES (batch/test) or the WORKDIR token(s) (interactive).
68	    pub positional: Vec<String>,
69	
70	    // -- proving / lemma selection (repeatable; None element = bare occurrence) --
71	    pub prove: Vec<Option<String>>,
72	    pub lemma: Vec<Option<String>>,
73	    pub defines: Vec<Option<String>>,
74	
75	    // -- value-validated flags --
76	    pub stop_on_trace: StopOnTrace,
77	    pub bound: Option<i64>,
78	    pub partial_evaluation: Option<PartialEval>,
79	    pub open_chains: i64,
80	    pub saturation: i64,
81	    pub output_module: Option<OutputModule>,
82	    pub derivcheck_timeout: i64,
83	    pub replication_bound: Option<i64>,
84	
85	    // -- unvalidated value flags (raw last-occurrence value) --
86	    pub heuristic: Option<String>,
87	    pub oraclename: Option<String>,
88	    pub output: Option<String>,
89	    pub output_dir: Option<String>,
90	    pub output_json: Option<String>,
91	    pub output_dot: Option<String>,
92	    pub with_dot: Option<String>,
93	    pub with_json: Option<String>,
94	    pub with_maude: Option<String>,
95	    // interactive-only value flags (unvalidated by the reference)
96	    pub port: Option<String>,
97	    pub interface: Option<String>,
98	    pub image_format: Option<String>,
99	
100	    // -- boolean flags --
101	    pub diff: bool,
102	    pub quit_on_warning: bool,
103	    pub auto_sources: bool,
104	    pub oracle_only: bool,
105	    pub quiet: bool,
106	    pub verbose: bool,
107	    pub no_reuse: bool,
108	    pub no_restrictions: bool,
109	    pub no_compress: bool,
110	    pub parse_only: bool,
111	    pub precompute_only: bool,
112	    pub debug: bool,
113	    pub no_logging: bool,
114	
115	    // -- consumer-extension flags (not in the reference, not in help) --
116	    pub processors: Option<u64>,
117	    pub maude_processes: Option<u64>,
118	    pub data_dir: Option<String>,
119	}
120	
121	/// The outcome of a typed parse: a help/version request, or a run with typed args.
122	///
123	/// `Run` is the common case and the value is consumed immediately, so the size
124	/// gap between variants carries no practical cost.
125	#[allow(clippy::large_enum_variant)]
126	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
127	pub enum Parsed {
128	    Help(Mode),
129	    Version,
130	    Run(Args),
131	}
132	
133	/// Typed parse of an argument vector (excluding argv[0]).
134	pub fn parse_args(argv: &[String]) -> Result<Parsed, ParseError> {
135	    match parse(argv)? {
136	        Command::Help(mode) => Ok(Parsed::Help(mode)),
137	        Command::Version => Ok(Parsed::Version),
138	        Command::Run(spec) => {
139	            let args = build_args(spec.mode, spec.positional, &spec.options)?;
140	            Ok(Parsed::Run(args))
141	        }
142	    }
143	}
144	
145	/// Resolve typed fields, validating values in the fixed precedence order so the
146	/// first invalid flag in that order produces the surfaced error.
147	fn build_args(mode: Mode, positional: Vec<String>, o: &Options) -> Result<Args, ParseError> {
148	    // Precedence order: the earliest invalid flag here is the one that errors.
149	    let stop_on_trace = stop_on_trace_field(o)?;
150	    let bound = int_opt(o, "bound", "bound")?;
151	    let partial_evaluation = partial_eval_field(o)?;
152	    let open_chains = int_default(o, "open-chains", "OpenChainsLimit", OPEN_CHAINS_DEFAULT)?;
153	    let saturation = int_default(o, "saturation", "SaturationLimit", SATURATION_DEFAULT)?;
154	    let output_module = output_module_field(o)?;
155	    let derivcheck_timeout =
156	        int_default(o, "derivcheck-timeout", "derivcheck-timeout", DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT)?;
157	    let replication_bound = int_opt(o, "replication-bound", "replication-bound")?;
158	
159	    // Consumer extensions (validated after all reference flags, so a reference
160	    // error always wins).
161	    let processors = positive_int_opt(o, "processors", "--processors")?;
162	    let maude_processes = positive_int_opt(o, "maude-processes", "--maude-processes")?;
163	
164	    Ok(Args {
165	        mode,
166	        positional,
167	        prove: o.occurrences("prove"),
168	        lemma: o.occurrences("lemma"),
169	        defines: o.occurrences("defines"),
170	        stop_on_trace,
171	        bound,
172	        partial_evaluation,
173	        open_chains,
174	        saturation,
175	        output_module,
176	        derivcheck_timeout,
177	        replication_bound,
178	        heuristic: raw_opt(o, "heuristic"),
179	        oraclename: raw_opt(o, "oraclename"),
180	        output: raw_opt(o, "output"),
181	        output_dir: raw_opt(o, "Output"),
182	        output_json: raw_opt(o, "output-json"),
183	        output_dot: raw_opt(o, "output-dot"),
184	        with_dot: raw_opt(o, "with-dot"),
185	        with_json: raw_opt(o, "with-json"),
186	        with_maude: raw_opt(o, "with-maude"),
187	        port: raw_opt(o, "port"),
188	        interface: raw_opt(o, "interface"),
189	        image_format: raw_opt(o, "image-format"),
190	        diff: o.is_set("diff"),
191	        quit_on_warning: o.is_set("quit-on-warning"),
192	        auto_sources: o.is_set("auto-sources"),
193	        oracle_only: o.is_set("oracle-only"),
194	        quiet: o.is_set("quiet"),
195	        verbose: o.is_set("verbose"),
196	        no_reuse: o.is_set("no-reuse"),
197	        no_restrictions: o.is_set("no-restrictions"),
198	        no_compress: o.is_set("no-compress"),
199	        parse_only: o.is_set("parse-only"),
200	        precompute_only: o.is_set("precompute-only"),
201	        debug: o.is_set("debug"),
202	        no_logging: o.is_set("no-logging"),
203	        processors,
204	        maude_processes,
205	        data_dir: raw_opt(o, "data-dir"),
206	    })
207	}
208	
209	/// Last-occurrence raw value (absent or bare → `None`).
210	fn raw_opt(o: &Options, long: &str) -> Option<String> {
211	    match o.last(long) {
212	        Some(Some(v)) => Some(v.to_string()),
213	        _ => None,
214	    }
215	}
216	
217	/// Integer flag with no documented default: `None` when absent/bare, else the
218	/// parsed value or the flag's `invalid bound` error.
219	fn int_opt(o: &Options, long: &str, label: &str) -> Result<Option<i64>, ParseError> {
220	    match o.last(long) {
221	        Some(Some(v)) => match read_haskell_int(v) {
222	            Some(n) => Ok(Some(n)),
223	            None => Err(errors::invalid_bound(label)),
224	        },
225	        _ => Ok(None),
226	    }
227	}
228	
229	/// Integer flag with a documented default applied for absent/bare occurrences.
230	fn int_default(o: &Options, long: &str, label: &str, default: i64) -> Result<i64, ParseError> {
231	    match o.last(long) {
232	        Some(Some(v)) => read_haskell_int(v).ok_or_else(|| errors::invalid_bound(label)),
233	        _ => Ok(default),
234	    }
235	}
236	
237	/// `--stop-on-trace`: case-insensitive; default DFS; invalid value echoes the
238	/// lowercased input.
239	fn stop_on_trace_field(o: &Options) -> Result<StopOnTrace, ParseError> {
240	    match o.last("stop-on-trace") {
241	        Some(Some(v)) => {
242	            let lower = v.to_ascii_lowercase();
243	            match lower.as_str() {
244	                "dfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Dfs),
245	                "bfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Bfs),
246	                "seqdfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::SeqDfs),
247	                "sorry" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Sorry),
248	                "none" => Ok(StopOnTrace::None),
249	                _ => Err(errors::unknown_stop_on_trace(&lower)),
250	            }
251	        }
252	        _ => Ok(StopOnTrace::Dfs),
253	    }
254	}
255	
256	/// `--partial-evaluation`: case-insensitive; no default; no value echoed on error.
257	fn partial_eval_field(o: &Options) -> Result<Option<PartialEval>, ParseError> {
258	    match o.last("partial-evaluation") {
259	        Some(Some(v)) => match v.to_ascii_lowercase().as_str() {
260	            "summary" => Ok(Some(PartialEval::Summary)),
261	            "verbose" => Ok(Some(PartialEval::Verbose)),
262	            _ => Err(errors::partial_evaluation_unknown()),
263	        },
264	        _ => Ok(None),
265	    }
266	}
267	
268	/// `-m --output-module`: case-SENSITIVE; no value echoed on error.
269	fn output_module_field(o: &Options) -> Result<Option<OutputModule>, ParseError> {
270	    match o.last("output-module") {
271	        Some(Some(v)) => match v {
272	            "spthytyped" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::SpthyTyped)),
273	            "spthy" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Spthy)),
274	            "msr" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Msr)),
275	            "proverifequiv" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::ProverifEquiv)),
276	            "proverif" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Proverif)),
277	            "deepsec" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Deepsec)),
278	            _ => Err(errors::output_mode_not_supported()),
279	        },
280	        _ => Ok(None),
281	    }
282	}
283	
284	/// A consumer-extension positive-integer flag: `None` when absent/bare, else a
285	/// value ≥ 1 or an original (non-reference) rejection.
286	fn positive_int_opt(o: &Options, long: &str, flag: &str) -> Result<Option<u64>, ParseError> {
287	    match o.last(long) {
288	        Some(Some(v)) => match v.trim().parse::<u64>() {
289	            Ok(n) if n >= 1 => Ok(Some(n)),
290	            _ => Err(errors::bad_positive_int(flag, v)),
291	        },
292	        _ => Ok(None),
293	    }
294	}
295	
296	/// Parse an integer the way Haskell's `readMaybe :: String -> Maybe Int` does, to
297	/// match the reference's accept/reject boundary (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §11):
298	/// trims surrounding whitespace, allows a leading `-` with optional whitespace
299	/// before the digits, accepts decimal / `0x`hex / `0o`octal, and rejects `+`, a
300	/// decimal point, underscores, trailing non-whitespace, and the empty string.
301	/// Values beyond the 64-bit range wrap rather than fail, mirroring `fromInteger`.
302	pub fn read_haskell_int(s: &str) -> Option<i64> {
303	    let t = s.trim();
304	    if t.is_empty() {
305	        return None;
306	    }
307	    let (negative, mag) = match t.strip_prefix('-') {
308	        Some(rest) => (true, rest.trim_start()),
309	        None => (false, t),
310	    };
311	    if mag.is_empty() {
312	        return None;
313	    }
314	    let (digits, radix) = if let Some(h) = mag.strip_prefix("0x").or_else(|| mag.strip_prefix("0X"))
315	    {
316	        (h, 16u32)
317	    } else if let Some(oc) = mag.strip_prefix("0o").or_else(|| mag.strip_prefix("0O")) {
318	        (oc, 8u32)
319	    } else {
320	        (mag, 10u32)
321	    };
322	    if digits.is_empty() {
323	        return None;
324	    }
325	    // Accumulate with wrapping so out-of-range inputs mirror Haskell's wrap rather
326	    // than being rejected.
327	    let mut acc: i64 = 0;
328	    for ch in digits.chars() {
329	        let d = ch.to_digit(radix)?;
330	        acc = acc.wrapping_mul(radix as i64).wrapping_add(d as i64);
331	    }
332	    Some(if negative { acc.wrapping_neg() } else { acc })
333	}

1	//! Error text renderers.
2	//!
3	//! Two tiers, all observed to exit 1, each pinned to a stream (see
4	//! `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §8, §10, §11):
5	//! - cmdargs-level parse errors, produced by [`crate::parse`] ([`ParseError`]):
6	//!   the bare one-liners (`Unknown flag`, `Ambiguous mode`, `Unhandled argument`)
7	//!   go to stderr; the `error: … + full help` validation envelopes go to stdout.
8	//! - value-validation errors and runtime errors printed with the maude preamble
9	//!   (integer/enum flag rejection, file-open failures, application `error` call
10	//!   sites) go to stderr.
11	//!
12	//! Every literal string here is compatibility output copied from observed oracle
13	//! output (see `workspace/captures/`), except the consumer-extension flag
14	//! rejections ([`bad_positive_int`], [`missing_value`]) which have no reference
15	//! and carry original text.
16	
17	use crate::help::render_help;
18	use crate::modes::Mode;
19	use crate::stream::Stream;
20	
21	/// A ready-to-print error: its text, the stream it is written to, and its exit
22	/// code (observed: always 1).
23	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
24	pub struct ParseError {
25	    pub text: String,
26	    pub stream: Stream,
27	    pub exit_code: i32,
28	}
29	
30	/// Legacy name retained for callers that predate the stream-aware split.
31	pub type CliError = ParseError;
32	
33	impl ParseError {
34	    fn new(text: String, stream: Stream) -> Self {
35	        ParseError { text, stream, exit_code: 1 }
36	    }
37	}
38	
39	// ---- cmdargs-level parse errors (bare one-liners -> stderr) -------------------
40	
41	/// `Unknown flag: <flag>` (`flag` includes its dashes).
42	pub fn unknown_flag(flag: &str) -> ParseError {
43	    ParseError::new(format!("Unknown flag: {}\n", flag), Stream::Err)
44	}
45	
46	/// `Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: <token>` for a value given to a
47	/// valueless flag (`token` is the whole `--flag=value`).
48	pub fn unhandled_argument(token: &str) -> ParseError {
49	    ParseError::new(
50	        format!("Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: {}\n", token),
51	        Stream::Err,
52	    )
53	}
54	
55	/// `Ambiguous mode '<token>', could be any of: <names…>`.
56	pub fn ambiguous_mode(token: &str, names: &[&str]) -> ParseError {
57	    ParseError::new(
58	        format!("Ambiguous mode '{}', could be any of: {}\n", token, names.join(" ")),
59	        Stream::Err,
60	    )
61	}
62	
63	// ---- cmdargs-level validation envelopes (message + full help -> stdout) -------
64	
65	/// `error: no input files given` + blank line + the mode's full help page.
66	pub fn no_input_files(mode: Mode) -> ParseError {
67	    ParseError::new(
68	        format!("error: no input files given\n\n{}", render_help(mode)),
69	        Stream::Out,
70	    )
71	}
72	
73	/// `error: directory '<dir>' does not exist.` + blank line + the mode's full help
74	/// page. (Interactive WORKDIR check; requires the filesystem, so it is a
75	/// caller-invoked renderer rather than a parse-time result.)
76	pub fn directory_does_not_exist(dir: &str, mode: Mode) -> ParseError {
77	    ParseError::new(
78	        format!("error: directory '{}' does not exist.\n\n{}", dir, render_help(mode)),
79	        Stream::Out,
80	    )
81	}
82	
83	// ---- value-validation errors (app `error` shape -> stderr) --------------------
84	
85	/// The application-`error` block: a message wrapped with the `tamarin-prover: `
86	/// prefix and the observed Haskell CallStack (every value-validation and known
87	/// application error cites the same call site).
88	pub fn app_error(message: &str) -> String {
89	    format!(
90	        "tamarin-prover: {}\nCallStack (from HasCallStack):\n  error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch\n",
91	        message
92	    )
93	}
94	
95	/// `<label>: invalid bound given` — a numeric flag given an unparseable value.
96	/// `label` is the flag's internal tag (`bound`, `OpenChainsLimit`, …).
97	pub fn invalid_bound(label: &str) -> ParseError {
98	    ParseError::new(app_error(&format!("{}: invalid bound given", label)), Stream::Err)
99	}
100	
101	/// `unknown stop-on-trace method: <value>` — the value is the lowercased input.
102	pub fn unknown_stop_on_trace(lowercased_value: &str) -> ParseError {
103	    ParseError::new(
104	        app_error(&format!("unknown stop-on-trace method: {}", lowercased_value)),
105	        Stream::Err,
106	    )
107	}
108	
109	/// `partial-evaluation: unknown option`.
110	pub fn partial_evaluation_unknown() -> ParseError {
111	    ParseError::new(app_error("partial-evaluation: unknown option"), Stream::Err)
112	}
113	
114	/// `output mode not supported.` — an unrecognized `--output-module` value.
115	pub fn output_mode_not_supported() -> ParseError {
116	    ParseError::new(app_error("output mode not supported."), Stream::Err)
117	}
118	
119	// ---- consumer-extension flag rejections (no reference; original text) ---------
120	
121	/// A consumer-extension flag (`--processors`, `--maude-processes`) whose value is
122	/// not a positive integer. This flag is not present in the reference binary, so
123	/// the text is original rather than reproduced.
124	pub fn bad_positive_int(flag: &str, value: &str) -> ParseError {
125	    ParseError::new(
126	        format!("error: flag {} expects a positive integer, got '{}'\n", flag, value),
127	        Stream::Err,
128	    )
129	}
130	
131	/// A consumer-extension flag given without the required value.
132	pub fn missing_value(flag: &str) -> ParseError {
133	    ParseError::new(format!("error: flag {} requires a value\n", flag), Stream::Err)
134	}
135	
136	// ---- runtime file-open errors (printed after the maude preamble -> stderr) ----
137	
138	/// Reason an input file could not be opened, with its exact message suffix.
139	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
140	pub enum OpenFileError {
141	    DoesNotExist,
142	    PermissionDenied,
143	    IsDirectory,
144	}
145	
146	impl OpenFileError {
147	    fn suffix(self) -> &'static str {
148	        match self {
149	            OpenFileError::DoesNotExist => "openFile: does not exist (No such file or directory)",
150	            OpenFileError::PermissionDenied => "openFile: permission denied (Permission denied)",
151	            OpenFileError::IsDirectory => "openFile: inappropriate type (is a directory)",
152	        }
153	    }
154	}
155	
156	/// `tamarin-prover: <path>: <reason>` — a file-open failure line (stderr).
157	pub fn open_file_error(path: &str, reason: OpenFileError) -> String {
158	    format!("tamarin-prover: {}: {}\n", path, reason.suffix())
159	}

1	//! Output-stream model.
2	//!
3	//! Every byte the binary emits is destined for exactly one of the two standard
4	//! streams. Which stream a given piece of text lands on is a fixed, probed
5	//! property (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §10): the theory payload, the summary
6	//! block, help pages, the version banner, and the `error: … + full help`
7	//! validation envelopes go to stdout; the maude preamble, per-theory progress
8	//! markers, the bare cmdargs one-liners, and all runtime errors go to stderr.
9	
10	/// One of the two standard output streams.
11	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
12	pub enum Stream {
13	    /// Standard output.
14	    Out,
15	    /// Standard error.
16	    Err,
17	}
18	
19	/// A pair of independently byte-exact stream buffers.
20	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, PartialEq, Eq)]
21	pub struct Streams {
22	    pub out: String,
23	    pub err: String,
24	}
25	
26	impl Streams {
27	    /// Append `text` to the buffer for `stream`.
28	    pub fn push(&mut self, stream: Stream, text: &str) {
29	        match stream {
30	            Stream::Out => self.out.push_str(text),
31	            Stream::Err => self.err.push_str(text),
32	        }
33	    }
34	}

1	//! Version banner and the maude readiness preamble.
2	//!
3	//! `--version`/`-V` writes to two streams (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §10): the
4	//! banner (tamarin line + warranty block + `Generated from:` block) goes to
5	//! stdout, and the maude readiness preamble goes to stderr. They only appeared
6	//! interleaved under the merged oracle. The stdout banner is stored as a template
7	//! with `{{SLOT}}` markers for the build/runtime metadata; the static text is
8	//! byte-exact observed output.
9	
10	use crate::stream::Streams;
11	
12	const VERSION_TEMPLATE: &str = include_str!("../fixtures/version.tmpl");
13	
14	/// Maude tool identity used in the readiness preamble.
15	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
16	pub struct MaudeInfo {
17	    /// Tool path as printed inside the quotes (default `maude`).
18	    pub path: String,
19	    /// Reported maude version, e.g. `3.5.1`.
20	    pub version: String,
21	}
22	
23	impl Default for MaudeInfo {
24	    fn default() -> Self {
25	        MaudeInfo { path: "maude".to_string(), version: "3.5.1".to_string() }
26	    }
27	}
28	
29	/// Build/runtime metadata that fills the version banner slots.
30	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
31	pub struct VersionInfo {
32	    pub maude: MaudeInfo,
33	    pub tamarin_version: String,
34	    pub copyright_years: String,
35	    /// Everything after `Git revision: ` (revision + dirty note + branch).
36	    pub git_description: String,
37	    /// The `Compiled at:` timestamp text.
38	    pub compiled_at: String,
39	}
40	
41	/// The stdout version banner, filling every slot in the observed template.
42	pub fn render_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> String {
43	    VERSION_TEMPLATE
44	        .replace("{{TAMARIN_VERSION}}", &info.tamarin_version)
45	        .replace("{{COPYRIGHT_YEARS}}", &info.copyright_years)
46	        .replace("{{MAUDE_VERSION}}", &info.maude.version)
47	        .replace("{{GIT_DESCRIPTION}}", &info.git_description)
48	        .replace("{{COMPILED_AT}}", &info.compiled_at)
49	}
50	
51	/// The full `--version`/`-V` output across both streams: the banner on stdout and
52	/// the maude readiness preamble on stderr.
53	pub fn frame_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> Streams {
54	    Streams { out: render_version(info), err: maude_preamble(&info.maude) }
55	}
56	
57	/// The 3-line maude readiness preamble emitted before batch/variants processing
58	/// (each line terminated by a newline).
59	pub fn maude_preamble(maude: &MaudeInfo) -> String {
60	    format!(
61	        "maude tool: '{}'\n checking version: {}. OK.\n checking installation: OK.\n",
62	        maude.path, maude.version
63	    )
64	}
65

1	//! Modes and their flag tables.
2	//!
3	//! The binary is a multi-mode command. The first token selects a mode by unique
4	//! prefix match against the mode names; otherwise the default (batch) mode is
5	//! used and the token is a positional. Each mode recognizes a different set of
6	//! flags, so the flag table is mode-scoped (a flag valid in one mode is
7	//! "Unknown" in another — e.g. `--version` outside the default mode).
8	
9	/// The four invocation modes.
10	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
11	pub enum Mode {
12	    /// Default mode when no mode token is given: analyze/prove theory FILES.
13	    Batch,
14	    /// `interactive` — web server; positional WORKDIR.
15	    Interactive,
16	    /// `variants` — compute intruder-rule variants; no positional.
17	    Variants,
18	    /// `test` — self-test; positional FILES.
19	    Test,
20	}
21	
22	impl Mode {
23	    /// The three named modes, in the order the help/ambiguity messages list them.
24	    pub const NAMED: [(&'static str, Mode); 3] = [
25	        ("interactive", Mode::Interactive),
26	        ("variants", Mode::Variants),
27	        ("test", Mode::Test),
28	    ];
29	
30	    /// Modes whose named token is a prefix of `token` (empty token matches all).
31	    pub fn prefix_matches(token: &str) -> Vec<(&'static str, Mode)> {
32	        Mode::NAMED
33	            .iter()
34	            .filter(|(name, _)| name.starts_with(token))
35	            .copied()
36	            .collect()
37	    }
38	}
39	
40	/// One recognized flag: its canonical long name, optional short char, whether it
41	/// accepts a value, any additional long aliases, and whether it consumes the
42	/// following token as its value.
43	///
44	/// `consumes_next` is `false` for every reference flag: the reference binary
45	/// never takes a space-separated value (`--bound 5` leaves `5` as a positional).
46	/// It is `true` only for the consumer-extension flags, whose surface is
47	/// `--flag <value>` and which are not present in the reference at all.
48	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
49	pub struct FlagSpec {
50	    pub long: &'static str,
51	    pub short: Option<char>,
52	    pub takes_value: bool,
53	    pub aliases: &'static [&'static str],
54	    pub consumes_next: bool,
55	}
56	
57	const fn boolf(long: &'static str, short: Option<char>) -> FlagSpec {
58	    FlagSpec { long, short, takes_value: false, aliases: &[], consumes_next: false }
59	}
60	const fn valf(long: &'static str, short: Option<char>) -> FlagSpec {
61	    FlagSpec { long, short, takes_value: true, aliases: &[], consumes_next: false }
62	}
63	/// A consumer-extension value flag that also accepts a space-separated value.
64	const fn extf(long: &'static str) -> FlagSpec {
65	    FlagSpec { long, short: None, takes_value: true, aliases: &[], consumes_next: true }
66	}
67	
68	// The proving/output flag subset shared by the default and interactive modes.
69	const PROVE_COMMON: &[FlagSpec] = &[
70	    valf("prove", None),
71	    valf("lemma", None),
72	    valf("stop-on-trace", None),
73	    valf("bound", Some('b')),
74	    valf("heuristic", None),
75	    valf("partial-evaluation", None),
76	    valf("defines", Some('D')),
77	    boolf("diff", None),
78	    boolf("quit-on-warning", None),
79	    boolf("auto-sources", None),
80	    valf("oraclename", None),
81	    boolf("oracle-only", None),
82	    boolf("quiet", None),
83	    boolf("verbose", Some('v')),
84	    valf("open-chains", Some('c')),
85	    valf("saturation", Some('s')),
86	    valf("derivcheck-timeout", Some('d')),
87	    boolf("no-reuse", None),
88	    boolf("no-restrictions", None),
89	    valf("replication-bound", None),
90	];
91	
92	const WITH_TOOLS: &[FlagSpec] = &[
93	    valf("with-dot", None),
94	    valf("with-json", None),
95	    valf("with-maude", None),
96	];
97	
98	/// Consumer-extension flags, recognized in the default/batch mode only and
99	/// deliberately absent from every help page. They accept both `--flag=value` and
100	/// the space-separated `--flag value` form.
101	pub const INTEROP_FLAGS: &[FlagSpec] = &[
102	    extf("processors"),
103	    extf("maude-processes"),
104	    extf("data-dir"),
105	];
106	
107	/// Flags recognized in the default/batch mode (excluding About flags).
108	pub fn batch_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
109	    let mut v: Vec<FlagSpec> = PROVE_COMMON.to_vec();
110	    v.extend_from_slice(&[
111	        boolf("no-compress", None),
112	        boolf("parse-only", None),
113	        boolf("precompute-only", None),
114	        valf("output", Some('o')),
115	        valf("Output", Some('O')),
116	        valf("output-module", Some('m')),
117	        FlagSpec {
118	            long: "output-json",
119	            short: None,
120	            takes_value: true,
121	            aliases: &["oj"],
122	            consumes_next: false,
123	        },
124	        FlagSpec {
125	            long: "output-dot",
126	            short: None,
127	            takes_value: true,
128	            aliases: &["od"],
129	            consumes_next: false,
130	        },
131	    ]);
132	    v.extend_from_slice(WITH_TOOLS);
133	    v.extend_from_slice(INTEROP_FLAGS);
134	    v
135	}
136	
137	/// Flags recognized in the interactive mode (excluding About flags).
138	pub fn interactive_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
139	    let mut v = vec![
140	        valf("port", Some('p')),
141	        valf("interface", Some('i')),
142	        valf("image-format", None),
143	        boolf("debug", None),
144	        boolf("no-logging", None),
145	    ];
146	    v.extend_from_slice(PROVE_COMMON);
147	    v.extend_from_slice(WITH_TOOLS);
148	    v
149	}
150	
151	/// Flags recognized in the variants mode (excluding About flags).
152	pub fn variants_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
153	    vec![valf("Output", Some('O'))]
154	}
155	
156	/// Flags recognized in the test mode (excluding About flags).
157	pub fn test_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
158	    WITH_TOOLS.to_vec()
159	}

161	/// The mode-scoped flag table (mode flags first, then About flags). `--version`
162	/// / `-V` is an About flag only in the default mode.
163	pub fn flags_for(mode: Mode) -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
164	    let mut v = match mode {
165	        Mode::Batch => batch_flags(),
166	        Mode::Interactive => interactive_flags(),
167	        Mode::Variants => variants_flags(),
168	        Mode::Test => test_flags(),
169	    };
170	    // About: help everywhere; version only in the default mode.
171	    v.push(boolf("help", Some('?')));
172	    if mode == Mode::Batch {
173	        v.push(boolf("version", Some('V')));
174	    }
175	    v
176	}

22	//!
23	//! What we currently support (batch / prove pipeline):
24	//!
25	//!   --prove[=LEMMA]            select a lemma (or prefix*) to prove. Repeatable.
26	//!   --lemma[=LEMMA]            (synonym for --prove without proving — kept for parity)
27	//!   --stop-on-trace=DFS|BFS|SEQDFS|SORRY|NONE

29	//!                              CutDFS default / CutBFS /

32	//!                              flag is absent the theory's in-file
33	//!                              `configuration:` block is consulted
34	//!                              (run.rs `effective_config`).  Without
35	//!                              --prove every value parses and is ignored,
36	//!                              as in HS.
37	//!   --bound=N, -bN             proof-depth bound

39	//!   --heuristic=...            heuristic ranking sequence (overrides per-lemma)

42	//!   --diff                     observational-equivalence mode (errors: not yet ported)

46	//!   --oracle-only              oracle-only mode (quit-on-empty-oracle)

322	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
323	        match self {
324	            CliError::Msg(m) => f.write_str(m),
325	        }
326	    }

329	impl std::error::Error for CliError {}
330	
331	/// Parse a raw argv-style slice (program name NOT included).
332	pub fn parse_args(raw: &[String]) -> Result<Args, CliError> {
333	    let mut args = Args::default();
334	
335	    // Subcommand detection: if the first non-option token is a known
336	    // subcommand name, route to it. Otherwise stay in batch mode.
337	    let mut i = 0;
338	    if let Some(first) = raw.first() {
339	        match first.as_str() {
340	            "interactive" => {
341	                args.subcommand = Subcommand::Interactive;
342	                i = 1;
343	            }
344	            "variants" => {
345	                args.subcommand = Subcommand::Variants;
346	                i = 1;
347	            }
348	            "test" | "test-prover" => {
349	                args.subcommand = Subcommand::Test;
350	                i = 1;
351	            }
352	            _ => {}
353	        }
354	    }
355	
356	    // A few short flags take a separate-token value (`-b N`, `-s N`,
357	    // `-o FILE`, etc.). We dispatch on full-name so all such cases
358	    // are explicit.
359	
360	    let mut positional: Vec<String> = Vec::new();
361	    while i < raw.len() {
362	        let a = &raw[i];
363	        if a == "--" {
364	            // Everything after is positional.
365	            for x in &raw[(i + 1)..] {
366	                positional.push(x.clone());
367	            }
368	            break;
369	        }
370	
371	        // Long flag.
372	        if let Some(rest) = a.strip_prefix("--") {
373	            let (key, val_inline) = split_eq(rest);
374	            match key {
375	                "help" => args.show_help = true,
376	                "version" => args.show_version = true,
377	                "prove" => {
378	                    args.prove_mode = true;
379	                    if let Some(v) = val_inline {
380	                        args.lemma_names.push(v.to_string());
381	                    } else {
382	                        // bare --prove → match-everything sentinel
383	                        args.lemma_names.push(String::new());
384	                    }
385	                }
386	                "lemma" => {
387	                    if let Some(v) = val_inline {
388	                        args.lemma_names.push(v.to_string());
389	                    } else {
390	                        args.lemma_names.push(String::new());
391	                    }
392	                }
393	                // flagOpt "dfs" — bare flag records the default "dfs", which
394	                // equals the Rust default; absent leaves None (same behaviour).
395	                "stop-on-trace" => {
396	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "dfs");
397	                    args.stop_on_trace = Some(StopOnTrace::parse(&v).map_err(CliError::Msg)?);
398	                }
399	                // flagOpt "5" (TheoryLoader.hs:105-110).  Load-bearing: HS
400	                // `proofBound = parseIntArg (findArg "bound") Nothing Just`, so
401	                // bare `--bound` records the default "5" => Some(5) (bounded
402	                // search) while an absent `--bound` stays None (unbounded).
403	                "bound" => {
404	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "5");
405	                    args.bound = Some(parse_int(&v, "bound")?);
406	                }
407	                "heuristic" => {
408	                    // flagOpt default = head of defaultRankings False; that
409	                    // default rebuilds the Rust default downstream, so a bare
410	                    // flag is behaviourally equal to absent — leave None.
411	                    // Routed: when set, this OVERRIDES the per-lemma / theory
412	                    // heuristic for every lemma (HS `selectHeuristic`:
413	                    // `apDefaultHeuristic <|> pcHeuristic`, Proof.hs:707).
414	                    if let Some(v) = val_inline {
415	                        args.heuristic = Some(v.to_string());
416	                    }
417	                }
418	                "partial-evaluation" => {
419	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "summary");
420	                    args.partial_evaluation =
421	                        Some(PartialEval::parse(&v).map_err(CliError::Msg)?);
422	                }
423	                "defines" => {
424	                    // flagOpt "" — bare `-D`/`--defines` records the empty
425	                    // string default (a no-op #define), matching HS.
426	                    args.defines.push(flag_opt(val_inline, ""));
427	                }
428	                "diff" => args.diff = true,
429	                "quit-on-warning" => args.quit_on_warning = true,
430	                "auto-sources" => args.auto_sources = true,
431	                "oraclename" => {
432	                    // Routed: sets the oracle relPath on every oracle ranking
433	                    // in the `--heuristic` chain (HS `mapOracleRanking
434	                    // (maybeSetOracleRelPath oraclename)`, TheoryLoader.hs:305).
435	                    // `Just "" -> Nothing` is handled at resolution time.
436	                    args.oracle_name = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, ""));
437	                }
438	                // Routed: `--oracle-only` sets quitOnEmpty on every oracle /
439	                // tactic ranking in the selected heuristic (HS `setQuitOnEmpty`,
440	                // Proof.hs:712-716).
441	                "oracle-only" => args.oracle_only = true,
442	                "quiet" => args.quiet = true,
443	                "verbose" => args.verbose = true,
444	                "open-chains" => {
445	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "10");
446	                    args.open_chains = Some(parse_int(&v, "open-chains")?);
447	                }
448	                "saturation" => {
449	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "5");
450	                    args.saturation = Some(parse_int(&v, "saturation")?);
451	                }
452	                "derivcheck-timeout" => {
453	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "5");
454	                    args.derivcheck_timeout = Some(parse_int(&v, "derivcheck-timeout")?);
455	                }
456	                "no-reuse" => args.no_reuse = true,
457	                "no-restrictions" => args.no_restrictions = true,
458	                "replication-bound" => {
459	                    let v = flag_opt(val_inline, "3");
460	                    args.replication_bound = Some(parse_int(&v, "replication-bound")?);
461	                }
462	                "no-compress" => args.no_compress = true,
463	                "parse-only" => args.parse_only = true,
464	                "precompute-only" => args.precompute_only = true,
465	                "processors" => {
466	                    args.processors =
467	                        Some(parse_positive_usize(&mut i, raw, val_inline, "processors")?);
468	                }
469	                "maude-processes" => {
470	                    args.maude_processes =
471	                        Some(parse_positive_usize(&mut i, raw, val_inline, "maude-processes")?);
472	                }
473	                // Output flags.  output / Output / output-module are flagOpt
474	                // (Batch.hs:76-78): only `=VALUE` or a bare flag (records the
475	                // default ""/""/"spthy"); they never consume the next token.
476	                "output" => {
477	                    args.output_file = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, ""));
478	                }
479	                // The long form in Haskell is `--Output` (capital O) for the
480	                // directory variant (Batch.hs:77 registers only `Output`/`O`);
481	                // there is no `--output-dir` alias, so it falls through to the
482	                // unknown-flag arm, exactly as HS does.
483	                "Output" => {
484	                    args.output_dir = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, ""));
485	                }
486	                "output-module" => {
487	                    args.output_module = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, "spthy"));
488	                }
489	                // output-json / output-dot are flagReq (Batch.hs:79-80): they
490	                // REQUIRE a value and DO consume a separate next token.
491	                "output-json" | "oj" => {
492	                    let v = take_val(&mut i, raw, val_inline, "output-json")?;
493	                    args.trace_json = Some(v);
494	                }
495	                "output-dot" | "od" => {
496	                    let v = take_val(&mut i, raw, val_inline, "output-dot")?;
497	                    args.trace_dot = Some(v);
498	                }
499	                // toolFlags are flagOpt (Environment.hs:31-33): defaults
500	                // maude/dot/json; bare flag records the default, no token.
501	                "with-maude" => {
502	                    args.maude_path = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, "maude"));
503	                }
504	                "with-dot" => {
505	                    args.dot_path = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, "dot"));
506	                }
507	                "with-json" => {
508	                    args.json_path = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, "json"));
509	                }
510	                // Interactive-mode flags are flagOpt (Interactive.hs:53-56),
511	                // so they never consume a separate token — only `=VALUE`.  A
512	                // bare flag records the empty-string default; HS then reads
513	                // port leniently (Interactive.hs:134-139, falls back to
514	                // defaultPort) and interface defaults to 127.0.0.1
515	                // (Interactive.hs:143), so an empty value behaves like absent.
516	                "port" => {
517	                    if let Some(v) = val_inline {
518	                        if !v.is_empty() {
519	                            args.port = Some(parse_int(v, "port")?);
520	                        }
521	                    }
522	                }
523	                "interface" => {
524	                    args.interface = Some(flag_opt(val_inline, ""));
525	                }
526	                "image-format" => {
527	                    if let Some(v) = val_inline {
528	                        if !v.is_empty() {
529	                            args.image_format =
530	                                Some(ImageFormat::parse(v).map_err(CliError::Msg)?);
531	                        }
532	                    }
533	                }
534	                "debug" => args.debug = true,
535	                "no-logging" => args.no_logging = true,
536	                "data-dir" => {
537	                    let v = take_val(&mut i, raw, val_inline, "data-dir")?;
538	                    args.data_dir = Some(v);
539	                }
540	                other => {
541	                    return Err(CliError::Msg(format!("unknown flag: --{}", other)));
542	                }
543	            }
544	            i += 1;
545	            continue;
546	        }
547	
548	        // Short flag(s). cmdargs in Haskell uses single-letter -X aliases.
549	        if let Some(rest) = a.strip_prefix('-') {
550	            if rest.is_empty() {
551	                // bare `-` — treat as positional (stdin convention)
552	                positional.push(a.clone());
553	                i += 1;
554	                continue;
555	            }
556	            // GNU-style clustering of boolean short flags, mirroring
557	            // System.Console.CmdArgs.Explicit (`-vh` sets both verbose
558	            // and help; HS `verbose`/`help`/`version` are all
559	            // no-argument `flagNone`/`flagHelpSimple`/`flagVersion`).
560	            // We walk the cluster char-by-char: a boolean short consumes
561	            // exactly one char and we continue with the rest; the first
562	            // value-taking short consumes the remainder of the token as
563	            // its inline value (e.g. `-b12`, `-vb12`) and ends the cluster.
564	            // All the value-taking shorts here are cmdargs `flagOpt`, so they
565	            // do NOT consume the next (space-separated) token; a bare short
566	            // (`-b`) records the flag's default.  Verified against the HS
567	            // binary: `-b 5 t.spthy` keeps `5` positional (`5: openFile: does
568	            // not exist`); `-b5` is inline; bare `-b` uses default 5.
569	            for (idx, key) in rest.char_indices() {
570	                // Bytes after this char in the token form a potential
571	                // inline value for a value-taking flag.  Strip a single
572	                // leading `=` to keep `-b=12` working.
573	                let after = &rest[idx + key.len_utf8()..];
574	                let inline_raw = after.strip_prefix('=').unwrap_or(after);
575	                let inline: Option<&str> =
576	                    if inline_raw.is_empty() { None } else { Some(inline_raw) };
577	                match key {
578	                    'h' | '?' => {
579	                        args.show_help = true;
580	                        continue;
581	                    }
582	                    'V' => {
583	                        args.show_version = true;
584	                        continue;
585	                    }
586	                    'v' => {
587	                        args.verbose = true;
588	                        continue;
589	                    }
590	                    'b' => {
591	                        let v = flag_opt(inline, "5");
592	                        args.bound = Some(parse_int(&v, "bound")?);
593	                    }
594	                    's' => {
595	                        let v = flag_opt(inline, "5");
596	                        args.saturation = Some(parse_int(&v, "saturation")?);
597	                    }
598	                    'c' => {
599	                        let v = flag_opt(inline, "10");
600	                        args.open_chains = Some(parse_int(&v, "open-chains")?);
601	                    }
602	                    'd' => {
603	                        let v = flag_opt(inline, "5");
604	                        args.derivcheck_timeout = Some(parse_int(&v, "derivcheck-timeout")?);
605	                    }
606	                    'D' => {
607	                        args.defines.push(flag_opt(inline, ""));
608	                    }
609	                    'o' => {
610	                        args.output_file = Some(flag_opt(inline, ""));
611	                    }
612	                    'O' => {
613	                        args.output_dir = Some(flag_opt(inline, ""));
614	                    }
615	                    'm' => {
616	                        args.output_module = Some(flag_opt(inline, "spthy"));
617	                    }
618	                    'p' => {
619	                        if let Some(v) = inline {
620	                            args.port = Some(parse_int(v, "port")?);
621	                        }
622	                    }
623	                    'i' => {
624	                        args.interface = Some(flag_opt(inline, ""));
625	                    }
626	                    other => {
627	                        return Err(CliError::Msg(format!("unknown short flag: -{}", other)));
628	                    }
629	                }
630	                // A value-taking flag consumed the remainder of the
631	                // token (and possibly the next token); stop scanning
632	                // this cluster.
633	                break;
634	            }
635	            i += 1;
636	            continue;
637	        }
638	
639	        // Positional.
640	        positional.push(a.clone());
641	        i += 1;
642	    }
643	    args.in_files = positional;
644	
645	    Ok(args)
646	}

648	impl Args {
649	    /// Resolve `--processors` (or its default).  Default = full
650	    /// machine parallelism (`available_parallelism()`); Maude IPC mutex
651	    /// contention is mediated by the `MaudePool` (`--maude-processes`),
652	    /// so every core can be used.
653	    pub fn effective_processors(&self) -> usize {
654	        match self.processors {
655	            Some(n) => n.max(1),
656	            None => std::thread::available_parallelism()
657	                .map(|p| p.get())
658	                .unwrap_or(1),
659	        }
660	    }
661	
662	    /// Resolve `--maude-processes` (or its default).
663	    ///
664	    /// Default = `max(1, effective_processors())` — a 1:1
665	    /// workers:maudes ratio.  Lemma-level parallelism (B1) plus the
666	    /// within-lemma fan-out both draw Maude handles from this pool
667	    /// concurrently, so a half-size pool would be exhausted, forcing the
668	    /// fan-out to fall back to the single shared subprocess (serialised
669	    /// IPC).  At 1:1 the
670	    /// Maude-bound theories keep more queries in flight (gcm ~-8%,
671	    /// Yubikey ~-12% @16c; output-identical — Maude is stateless so pool
672	    /// size never affects results).  Costs ~30-100 MB per extra
673	    /// subprocess on real protocols; tight-RAM users can dial it back
674	    /// with `--maude-processes=N`.  Going ABOVE `procs` over-subscribes
675	    /// and regresses (measured), so do not raise the default further.
676	    ///
677	    /// When `--processors=1`, force pool size 1 (no parallelism to
678	    /// exploit; saves spawn cost).
679	    pub fn effective_maude_processes(&self) -> usize {
680	        let procs = self.effective_processors();
681	        if procs == 1 {
682	            return 1;
683	        }
684	        match self.maude_processes {
685	            Some(n) => n.max(1),
686	            None => procs.max(1),
687	        }
688	    }
689	}

691	fn split_eq(s: &str) -> (&str, Option<&str>) {
692	    match s.find('=') {
693	        Some(i) => (&s[..i], Some(&s[(i + 1)..])),
694	        None => (s, None),
695	    }
696	}

698	/// Resolve a `cmdargs` `flagOpt` value: the inline `=VALUE` if present,
699	/// otherwise the flag's documented default string (recorded when the bare
700	/// flag is given).  A `flagOpt` flag NEVER consumes a separate following
701	/// token — that token stays positional.  This mirrors
702	/// `System.Console.CmdArgs.Explicit.flagOpt`, used by HS for `--bound`,
703	/// `--output`, `--with-maude`, etc.  Verified against the installed HS
704	/// binary: `tamarin-prover --bound 5 t.spthy` treats `5` as a positional
705	/// file (`5: openFile: does not exist`), while bare `--bound` uses default 5.
706	fn flag_opt(inline: Option<&str>, default: &str) -> String {
707	    inline.unwrap_or(default).to_string()
708	}
709	
710	fn take_val(
711	    i: &mut usize,
712	    raw: &[String],
713	    inline: Option<&str>,
714	    name: &str,
715	) -> Result<String, CliError> {
716	    if let Some(v) = inline {
717	        return Ok(v.to_string());
718	    }
719	    let next = raw.get(*i + 1).cloned().ok_or_else(|| {
720	        CliError::Msg(format!("flag --{} requires a value", name))
721	    })?;
722	    if next.starts_with('-') {
723	        return Err(CliError::Msg(format!(
724	            "flag --{} requires a value (got {:?})",
725	            name, next
726	        )));
727	    }
728	    *i += 1;
729	    Ok(next)
730	}

732	fn parse_int<T: std::str::FromStr>(s: &str, name: &str) -> Result<T, CliError> {
733	    s.parse::<T>().map_err(|_| {
734	        CliError::Msg(format!("{}: expected integer, got {:?}", name, s))
735	    })
736	}

738	/// Take a flag's value, parse it as a `usize`, and reject `0` with a
739	/// `--<name> must be >= 1` error.  Shared by the `--processors` and
740	/// `--maude-processes` arms, which are otherwise identical.
741	fn parse_positive_usize(
742	    i: &mut usize,
743	    raw: &[String],
744	    inline: Option<&str>,
745	    name: &str,
746	) -> Result<usize, CliError> {
747	    let v = take_val(i, raw, inline, name)?;
748	    let n: usize = parse_int(&v, name)?;
749	    if n == 0 {
750	        return Err(CliError::Msg(format!("--{} must be >= 1", name)));
751	    }
752	    Ok(n)
753	}

755	/// Does the lemma name match the user's `--prove`/`--lemma` filter?
756	///
757	/// Mirrors HS `lemmaSelector` (TheoryLoader.hs:378-389): the empty
758	/// filter `[]`, the single-empty filter `[""]`, and the double-empty
759	/// filter `["",""]` all mean "all lemmas".  Otherwise we run
760	/// `any lemmaMatches filter` where a pattern ending in `*` matches by
761	/// prefix (with the `*` dropped) and any other pattern (including a
762	/// bare `""`) matches only by exact name.  Note this is NOT "drop all
763	/// empties": three or more bare entries (e.g. `["","",""]`) fall
764	/// through to the `any` arm and match nothing, exactly like HS.
765	pub fn lemma_matches(filter: &[String], lemma_name: &str) -> bool {
766	    match filter.len() {
767	        0 => return true,
768	        1 if filter[0].is_empty() => return true,
769	        2 if filter[0].is_empty() && filter[1].is_empty() => return true,
770	        _ => {}
771	    }
772	    filter.iter().any(|pat| {
773	        if let Some(prefix) = pat.strip_suffix('*') {
774	            lemma_name.starts_with(prefix)
775	        } else {
776	            pat == lemma_name
777	        }
778	    })
779	}

794	// [`adapt::version_streams`], which returns both standard streams: the banner on
795	// stdout and the maude readiness preamble on stderr, byte-exact against the HS
796	// split-stream captures (`split_version.out.txt` / `.err.txt`). The round-4 clean
797	// model corrected the earlier merged form that bundled the maude self-check lines
798	// into stdout.

800	/// Probe `maude --version` on `PATH` and return the trimmed version
801	/// string when Maude is reachable, `None` otherwise.
802	///
803	/// Mirrors HS `maudePath = fromMaybe "maude" . findArg "withMaude"`
804	/// (Console.hs:84-85): when no `--with-maude` is supplied, HS probes the
805	/// bare `maude` binary on `PATH` — it never consults hardcoded
806	/// developer-box paths.  Use [`detect_maude_version_at`] to honor an
807	/// explicit `--with-maude` path.
808	pub fn detect_maude_version_pub() -> Option<String> {
809	    detect_maude_version_at("maude")
810	}
811	
812	/// Probe `<path> --version` and return the trimmed version string when
813	/// the binary is reachable, `None` otherwise.  A caller that has an
814	/// explicit `--with-maude` path can pass it here so the reported version
815	/// matches the binary the prover will actually invoke (HS `ensureMaude`
816	/// uses `maudePath`).  No current caller does — the only caller is
817	/// [`detect_maude_version_pub`], which probes the bare `maude` binary.
818	pub fn detect_maude_version_at(path: &str) -> Option<String> {
819	    if let Ok(out) = std::process::Command::new(path).arg("--version").output() {
820	        if out.status.success() {
821	            let s = String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stdout);
822	            // Maude prints just the version number, e.g. "3.5.1".
823	            let v = s.trim().to_string();
824	            if !v.is_empty() {
825	                return Some(v);
826	            }
827	        }
828	    }
829	    None
830	}

26	//! Batch-mode driver: turn parsed [`Args`] into proof attempts and
27	//! produce an analyzed-theory output document.
28	//!
29	//! Mirrors `Main.Mode.Batch.run` in spirit — load each input file,
30	//! parse + elaborate, optionally prove lemmas, and emit either to
31	//! stdout or to `--output=` / `-O DIR`. The analyzed-theory output is
32	//! rendered via `pretty_theory::pretty_closed_theory`, the port of
33	//! Haskell's `prettyClosedTheory`, which interleaves the theory items
34	//! with their per-lemma proof/summary annotations.
35	//!
36	//! `--parse-only` is the one path that re-emits the source verbatim
37	//! (no analysis); all other modes go through the pretty-printer.

41	// byte-parity surface itself).  `println!`/`print!` are the intended output
42	// mechanism here, so the `disallowed_macros` convention freeze is allowed for
43	// this file.  (Library crates stay guarded; only the binary's output paths and
44	// examples carry this allow.)

73	/// `summary of summaries:` block.
74	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
75	pub enum LemmaVerdict {
76	    Verified,
77	    Falsified,
78	    /// We exhausted the search budget or hit `Sorry`.
79	    Analyzed,
80	    /// HS `UnfinishableProof`: no open goals but subterm store has reducible
81	    /// operators.  HS `showProofStatus` (Theory/Proof.hs:1109):
82	    ///   "analysis cannot be finished (reducible operators in subterms)"
83	    Unfinishable,
84	    /// HS `UndeterminedProof` (Theory/Proof.hs:1111): proof tree folds to a
85	    /// status that could not be determined — renders "analysis undetermined".
86	    Undetermined,
87	    /// HS `InvalidatedProof` (Theory/Proof.hs:1112): a stored proof step was
88	    /// invalidated (e.g. by an interactive reuse-lemma edit) — renders
89	    /// "proof has been invalidated".
90	    Invalidated,
91	    /// `[reuse]`-only lemma that we didn't try to prove (out of filter).
92	    Skipped,
93	    /// Lemma was filtered out by `--prove=FOO` / `--lemma=FOO`.
94	    Filtered,
95	    Error(String),
96	}

98	/// HS-faithful per-lemma summary line, mirroring `prettyClosedSummary`
99	/// (ClosedTheory.hs:463, which renders `showProofStatus ... <-> (siz "steps")`):
100	///   `<lemma> (<quantifier>): falsified - found trace (<N> steps)`
101	///   `<lemma> (<quantifier>): verified (<N> steps)`
102	///   `<lemma> (<quantifier>): analysis incomplete (<N> steps)`
103	///   `<lemma> (<quantifier>): analysis cannot be finished (reducible operators in subterms) (<N> steps)`
104	fn format_lemma_summary_line(r: &LemmaResult) -> String {
105	    let quantifier = if r.exists_trace { "exists-trace" } else { "all-traces" };
106	    let body = match &r.verdict {
107	        // HS `showProofStatus` (Theory/Proof.hs:1105-1108): a falsified
108	        // exists-trace lemma is a `CompleteProof` of `ExistsSomeTrace`
109	        // ("falsified - no trace found"), whereas a falsified all-traces
110	        // lemma is a `TraceFound` for `ExistsNoTrace` ("falsified - found
111	        // trace").  The wording therefore depends on the quantifier.
112	        LemmaVerdict::Falsified if r.exists_trace =>
113	            format!("falsified - no trace found ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
114	        LemmaVerdict::Falsified => format!("falsified - found trace ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
115	        LemmaVerdict::Verified => format!("verified ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
116	        LemmaVerdict::Analyzed
117	        | LemmaVerdict::Skipped
118	        | LemmaVerdict::Filtered => format!("analysis incomplete ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
119	        // HS `showProofStatus _ UnfinishableProof` (Theory/Proof.hs:1109).
120	        LemmaVerdict::Unfinishable =>
121	            format!("analysis cannot be finished (reducible operators in subterms) ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
122	        // HS `showProofStatus _ UndeterminedProof` (Theory/Proof.hs:1111).
123	        LemmaVerdict::Undetermined =>
124	            format!("analysis undetermined ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
125	        // HS `showProofStatus _ InvalidatedProof` (Theory/Proof.hs:1112).
126	        LemmaVerdict::Invalidated =>
127	            format!("proof has been invalidated ({} steps)", r.proof_steps),
128	        LemmaVerdict::Error(msg) => format!("error: {}", msg),
129	    };
130	    format!("{} ({}): {}", r.name, quantifier, body)
131	}

158	/// Top-level dispatch. Reports any error as a `RunError` and returns
159	/// the exit code the binary should use (0 for success).
160	pub fn run(args: &Args) -> Result<i32, RunError> {
161	    if args.show_help {
162	        // Per-mode HS help page, rendered by the vendored clean `help` module.
163	        print!("{}", crate::cli::adapt::help_text(&args.subcommand));
164	        return Ok(0);
165	    }
166	    if args.show_version {
167	        // The round-4 clean `version` model splits what the merged (2>&1) oracle
168	        // interleaved: the banner is stdout, the maude readiness preamble is
169	        // stderr (byte-exact vs `split_version.out.txt` / `.err.txt`). Both
170	        // buffers carry their own trailing newlines.
171	        let v = crate::cli::adapt::version_streams();
172	        print!("{}", v.out);
173	        eprint!("{}", v.err);
174	        return Ok(0);
175	    }
176	
177	    match args.subcommand {
178	        Subcommand::Batch => run_batch(args),
179	        Subcommand::Interactive => run_interactive(args),
180	        Subcommand::Variants => run_variants(args),
181	        Subcommand::Test => run_test(args),
182	    }
183	}

185	/// `tamarin-prover test` — mirror HS's installation self-test
186	/// (`Main.Mode.Test`).  HS runs:
187	///   1. Maude version check.
188	///   2. GraphViz `dot` version check.
189	///   3. The Haskell unit-test suite (55 cases as of v1.13.0).
190	///
191	/// We do (1) and (2) here.  Porting the unit test suite is a separate
192	/// effort; until then we run the prover's own lib tests at build time
193	/// instead (`cargo test`).  Returns rc=0 on Maude/dot reachable,
194	/// rc=1 otherwise.
195	fn run_test(_args: &Args) -> Result<i32, RunError> {
196	    println!("Self-testing the tamarin-prover installation.\n");
197	    println!("*** Testing the availability of the required tools ***");
198	    let mv = crate::cli::detect_maude_version_pub();
199	    match &mv {
200	        Some(v) => println!("{}. OK.\n checking installation: OK.", v),
201	        None => {
202	            eprintln!("Maude check FAILED — not found on $PATH.");
203	            return Ok(1);

205	    }
206	    let dot = std::process::Command::new("dot").arg("-V").output();
207	    // HS `successGraphVizDot = isJust maybeSuccessGraphVizDot` (Test.hs:51):
208	    // a missing/unavailable `dot` is a test FAILURE, not a silent skip.
209	    let success_graphviz = match dot {
210	        Ok(out) if out.status.success() => {
211	            let s = String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr);
212	            println!("GraphViz tool: 'dot'\n checking version: {}OK.", s.trim());
213	            true
214	        }
215	        _ => {
216	            println!("GraphViz check skipped (`dot` not found).");
217	            false
218	        }
219	    };
220	    println!("\n*** TEST SUMMARY ***");
221	    // HS `success = successMaude && successGraphVizDot && successTerm`
222	    // (Test.hs:96); on failure it warns and `exitFailure` (Test.hs:97-105).
223	    // Maude reachability is asserted above (early `Ok(1)` return), so the
224	    // only failure reachable here is a missing GraphViz `dot`.
225	    if success_graphviz {
226	        println!("All tool checks successful.");
227	        println!("The tamarin-prover should work as intended.\n");
228	        println!("           :-) happy proving (-:");
229	        Ok(0)
230	    } else {
231	        println!("\nWARNING: Some tests failed.");
232	        println!("The tamarin-prover might NOT WORK AS INTENDED.\n");
233	        Ok(1)
234	    }
